numeric_label_policy.disputed_policy: DISPUTED is excluded, never mapped to NOT_ENTITLED.

original_row.claim: While changes in cloud cover & aerosols lead to more sunlight hitting the surface, this can be compensated by the cooling effect on the atmosphere due to fewer clouds trapping less warmth and fewer absorbing aerosols absorbing less sunlight.

evidences.evidence: Some primarily scatter sunlight and thereby cool the planet, while others absorb sunlight and warm the atmosphere.

The particles reflect and absorb sunlight, less sun rays reach Earth surface layers, thus resulting in cooler water and land surface temperatures, and also less cloud formation, subsequently dampening the development of hurricanes.
If cloud cover increases, more sunlight will be reflected back into space, cooling the planet.
For instance, the change from a dark forest to grassland makes the surface lighter, causing it to reflect more sunlight.
For instance, if absorbing aerosols are present in a layer aloft in the atmosphere, they can heat surrounding air which inhibits the condensation of water vapour, resulting in less cloud formation.

original_row.claim: Climate Models Have Overestimated Global Warming

evidences.evidence: These models predict an upward trend in the global mean surface temperature, with the most rapid increase in temperature being projected for the higher latitudes of the Northern Hemisphere.
Uncertainty over feedbacks is the major reason why different climate models project different magnitudes of warming for a given amount of emissions.
Past models have underestimated the rate of Arctic shrinkage and underestimated the rate of precipitation increase.
The 2017 United States-published National Climate Assessment notes that "climate models may still be underestimating or missing relevant feedback processes".
Lindzen said that predicted warming may be overestimated because of their handling of the climate system's water vapor feedback.

sample_rejected_rows.detected_claim_snippet: Climate Models Have Overestimated Global Warming
sample_rejected_rows.detected_evidence_snippet: These models predict an upward trend in the global mean surface temperature, with the most rapid increase in temperature being projected for the higher latitudes of the Northern Hemisphere.

original_row.claim: Whatever is driving increases in winter Arctic temperatures   is not heat coming out of the Arctic Ocean, which is covered with   insulating ice.'

evidences.evidence: This insulation keeps the warm Atlantic Water from melting the surface ice.
Much of the Arctic Ocean is covered by sea ice that varies in extent and thickness seasonally.
In the winter the relatively warm ocean water exerts a moderating influence, even when covered by ice.
It is partly covered by sea ice throughout the year and almost completely in winter.
Sea ice has an important effect on the heat balance of the polar oceans, since it insulates the (relatively) warm ocean from the much colder air above, thus reducing heat loss from the oceans.

sample_rejected_rows.detected_claim_snippet: Whatever is driving increases in winter Arctic temperatures   is not heat coming out of the Arctic Ocean, which is covered with   insulating ice.'
sample_rejected_rows.detected_evidence_snippet: This insulation keeps the warm Atlantic Water from melting the surface ice.

original_row.claim: Hurricanes, tornadoes, floods, droughts and other natural disasters have yet to show any obvious long-term change.”

evidences.evidence: Climate change, through rising temperatures, changing rainfall patterns, and changing sea levels, will affect the nature of hydrometeorological disasters, such as droughts, floods, and cyclones.
There may have been changes in other climate extremes (e.g., floods, droughts and tropical cyclones) but these changes are more difficult to identify.
Researchers have for the first time attributed recent floods, droughts and heat waves, to human-induced climate change.

A natural disaster is a major adverse event resulting from natural processes of the Earth; examples are floods, hurricanes, tornadoes, volcanic eruptions, earthquakes, tsunamis, storms, and other geologic processes.
Another form of severe weather is drought, which is a prolonged period of persistently dry weather (that is, absence of precipitation).

sample_rejected_rows.detected_claim_snippet: Hurricanes, tornadoes, floods, droughts and other natural disasters have yet to show any obvious long-term change.”
sample_rejected_rows.detected_evidence_snippet: Climate change, through rising temperatures, changing rainfall patterns, and changing sea levels, will affect the nature of hydrometeorological disasters, such as droughts, floods, and cyclones.

original_row.claim: When accelerating ice loss from Greenland and Antarctica are factored into sea level projections, the estimated sea level rise by 2100 is between 75cm to 2 metres.

evidences.evidence: Estimates on future contribution to sea level rise from Greenland range from 0.3 to 3 metres (1 to 10 ft), for the year 2100.
According to the Fourth (2017) National Climate Assessment (NCA) of the United States it is very likely sea level will rise between 30 and 130 cm (1.0–4.3 feet) in 2100 compared to the year 2000.
In 2019, a study projected that in low emission scenario, sea level will rise 30 centimeters by 2050 and 69 centimetres by 2100, relatively to the level in 2000.
In high emission scenario, it will be 34 cm by 2050 and 111 cm by 2100.

For example, in 2007 the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) projected a high end estimate of 60 cm (2 ft) through 2099, but their 2014 report raised the high-end estimate to about 90 cm (3 ft).

sample_rejected_rows.detected_evidence_snippet: Estimates on future contribution to sea level rise from Greenland range from 0.3 to 3 metres (1 to 10 ft), for the year 2100.

risks: HuggingFace source schema or label metadata may change over time.
Acquired rows must remain external-evaluation-only under the leakage policy.
Climate-FEVER DISPUTED rows are excluded for clean 3-way evaluation.
recommendation: Accepted rows cover SUPPORT, REFUTE, and NOT_ENTITLED. The file is ready for future Stage43-B2 eval-only use.
leakage_policy: Stage43-B1 acquired data is external-evaluation-only. It must not be used for training, calibration, threshold selection, checkpoint selection, loss design, or any other model-selection feedback loop.
